revision.comment: Bot: mark 1 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24487645413|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漢鏡]]

{{nihongo|'''Han-style mirror'''|漢鏡|Kankyou|Hàn jìng}}) refers to a general term for {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirrors|1=Bronze mirror}} produced during the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}} (202 BCE–220 CE). Most were made of {{ill|Cupronickel|en|Cupronickel|qid=Q649090|lt=Cupronickel|1=Cupronickel}}, {{Refnest|group=note|In modern times, white bronze is defined as an alloy of [[copper]] and [[nickel]]. However, prior to this, white bronze referred to a type of {{ill|Bronze|en|Bronze|qid=Q34095|lt=bronze|1=Bronze}} with a particularly high tin content of 25–30%. This definition is still often used in historical disciplines like {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}}. As the tin content in bronze increases, its color shifts from a reddish hue, like that of a {{ill|Japanese 10-yen coin|ja|10円硬貨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese 10-yen coin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十円硬貨|1=10 yen coin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4546963}}, to a silvery-white, while also becoming harder and more brittle.{{sfn|Tanabe Yoshikazu|1971|p=445}}}} and were primarily used as {{ill|cosmetic tools|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic tools|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Makeup|qid=Q1190910}}.  

The mirrors were predominantly circular. Early Han mirrors often featured flat surfaces, but convex mirrors became more prevalent in later periods. The reverse side of the mirrors (the side opposite the reflective surface) was often adorned with patterns and inscriptions reflecting the cosmological and {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|ja|神仙思想|lt=immortality beliefs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=Xian (Taoism)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1207451}} of the time. {{Refnest|group=note|Immortality beliefs involved the faith in the existence of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|qid=Q1207451|lt=immortals|1=Xian (Taoism)}} and the aspiration to become immortal through specific practices.{{sfn|Kotobank: Immortality Beliefs}}}} These patterns and inscriptions, along with their classifications into "mirror styles," are valuable in {{ill|Absolute dating|en|Absolute dating|qid=Q332423|lt=archaeological dating|1=Absolute dating}} for estimating the periods of relics and ruins. Mirrors with inscriptions bearing {{ill|era names|ja|元号|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/era names|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Era name|qid=Q256408}}, known as "dated inscription mirrors" (紀年銘鏡), are particularly significant for chronological studies. Additionally, the study of the imagery and inscriptions on Kan mirrors is of great interest in Chinese {{ill|Intellectual_history|en|Intellectual_history|qid=Q1195695|lt=intellectual history|1=Intellectual history}} and {{ill|History_of_literature|en|History_of_literature|qid=Q6497044|lt=literary history|1=History of literature}}.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|1984|p=661}}{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=44-46}}  

Kan mirrors, thought to have been gifted by the Han court to tributary states as reciprocal gifts, have been unearthed across a wide area of East Asia, including the {{ill|Japanese archipelago|en|Japanese archipelago|qid=Q841337|lt=Japanese archipelago|1=Japanese archipelago}}, {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|qid=Q483134}}, {{ill|Vietnam|en|Vietnam|qid=Q881|lt=Vietnam|1=Vietnam}}, and {{ill|Central Asia|en|Central Asia|qid=Q27275|lt=Central Asia|1=Central Asia}}.{{sfn|Morishita Akihiko|2019|p=13}} This distribution reflects the extent of the Han dynasty's interaction network. {{Refnest|group=note|The westernmost site where Kan mirrors have been found is the Tillya Tepe archaeological site in {{ill|Afghanistan|en|Afghanistan|qid=Q889|lt=Afghanistan|1=Afghanistan}}.{{sfn|Morishita Akihiko|2019|p=25-28}}}} In the Japanese archipelago, Kan mirrors were likely introduced through {{ill|Lelang Commandery|en|Lelang Commandery|qid=Q701376|lt=Lelang Commandery|1=Lelang Commandery}} on the Korean Peninsula and are found in sites dating from the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} to the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. While many were interred as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=grave goods|1=Grave goods}} for prominent individuals, they have also been excavated from settlement and {{ill|ritual sites|ja|祭祀遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ritual sites|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135504575}}. These mirrors are believed to have been used as {{ill|prestige goods|ja|威信財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prestige goods|qq=}} or {{ill|amulets|ja|呪具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/amulets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Tsujita Junichirō|2019|p=8-9}}{{sfn|Morishita Akihiko|2019|p=19-20}}  

Even after the fall of the Han dynasty, imitation Kan mirrors continued to be produced, influencing later bronze mirrors. The term '''Kan-style mirrors''' (漢式鏡) broadly encompasses mirrors bearing the characteristics of Kan mirrors without restricting the time or place of production, and this article also touches on these.{{sfn|Kotobank: Kan-style mirrors}}  

==See Also==

* {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daryu Mirror|qq=}}
* {{ill|Wa-produced mirrors|ja|倭製鏡|1=Bōseikyō|lt=Wa-produced mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wa-produced mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōseikyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仿製鏡}}
*

* {{ill|Shinju-kyo|lt=Mirrors with images of deities and ...sed mirrors|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinju-kyo|qq=}}
* [[Shikinzan Kofun]]   

== Notes ==  
=== Annotations ===  
{{Notelist|2}}  
=== References ===  
{{Reflist|20em}}  

== Bibliography ==  

;Books  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Okamura Hidenori|authorlink=岡村秀典 |title=The Era of Sankaku-en Shinjukyō|year=1999 |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |series=Historical and Cultural Library 66|isbn=4-642-05466-9 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Okamura Hidenori |title=History Told by Mirrors|year=2017 |publisher=[[Iwanami Shoten]] |series=Iwanami Shinsho|isbn=978-4-00-431664-0 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Shitagaki Hitoshi|authorlink=下垣仁志 |title=The Formation of States in the Kofun Period|year=2018 |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |isbn=978-4-642-09352-1 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Educational Board of Takamatsu City |title=Report on the Investigation of Tsuruo Shrine Mound No. 4|year=1983 |publisher=Educational Board of Takamatsu City |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Tanaka Taku|authorlink=田中琢 |title=The Complete Series of Japanese Primitive Art: Bells, Swords, and Mirrors|year=1977 |editor=Tanaka Taku |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |volume=4 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Terasawa Kaoru|authorlink=寺沢薫 |chapter=Chronological Dating of the Yayoi Period Based on Archaeological Materials|title=Survey of Archaeological Materials|volume=Vol. 10 - Ruins and Structures: Yayoi and Kofun Periods|editor=Terasawa Kaoru |year=2004 |publisher=[[Shogakukan]]|ISBN=4-09-699760-9 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Tsujita Junichirō |title=Ancient History of Mirrors|year=2019 |publisher=[[KADOKAWA]] |series=Kadokawa Selections|isbn=978-4-04-703663-5 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Fukunaga Nobuya |chapter=From Bells to Mirrors|title=How Ancient States Were Born|editor=Tode Hiroshi|editor-link=都出比呂志 |year=1998 |publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|ISBN=4-04-821056-4|ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Minami Kentarō |title=Bronze Mirrors and Yayoi Society in East Asia|year=2019 |publisher=[[Dōsei Publishing]] |isbn=978-4-88621-819-3 |ref=harv}}  

;Articles and Papers  
* {{Cite journal |和書 |author=Ueno Yoshifumi |title=Transformations in the Distribution System of Chinese Mirrors in the Japanese Archipelago|journal=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|volume=185 |publisher=[[National Museum of Japanese History]] |year=2014 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite journal |和書 |author=Okamura Hidenori |title=Chronology and Style of Han Mirrors|journal=Shirin|volume=67-5 |publisher=Shigaku Kenkyūkai |year=1984 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite journal |和書 |author=Okamura Hidenori |title=A Thousand Years of Mirror Research|journal=Tōyōshi Kenkyū|volume=69-4 |publisher=[[Society of Oriental Studies]] |year=20dd |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite journal |和書 |author=Terasawa Kaoru |title=Chronological and Contextual Analysis of Yayoi Period Mirrors|journal=Ancient Studies|volume=169 |publisher=Association for Ancient Studies |year=2005 |ref=harv}}  
* {{Cite journal |和書 |author=Nishimura Toshinori |title=Issues with Han Mirrors|journal=Human Culture Studies|volume=29 |publisher=[[Kyoto University of Advanced Science]] |year=2012 |ref=harv}}  

;Dictionaries and Other Sources  
* “[https://kotobank.jp/ Kotobank]”. {{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|qid=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}}, [[CARTA HOLDINGS|VOYAGE MARKETING]].  
''' {{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%BC%A2%E9%8F%A1-48580 |title=Han Mirror |accessdate=2021-09-22|ref={{sfnref|Kotobank: Han Mirror}}}} (Excerpted from the {{ill|Encyclopedia Britannica|ja|ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia Britannica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Britannica International Encyclopedia|qid=Q109359966}}).
